visual_notes: Fire opal's vivid orange-to-red body color makes it the only facetable opal variety (when transparent). The color evokes live flame, molten metal, and sunset. In Mexico it is called 'piedra de fuego' (stone of fire) and was sacred to the Aztecs as a symbol of Huitzilopochtli.
prompt_fragments: transparent Mexican fire opal — concentrated sunlight in amorphous silica
Querétaro orange — volcanic glass holding warm flame
Aztec sacred fire-bird stone in vivid orange
vibe_notes: Fire opal is concentrated solar energy in amorphous silica — orange-red volcanic glass that holds the color of fire without being consumed by it. It has none of the spectral mystery of black opal; instead it confronts you with pure warm chromatic intensity.
folklore_summary: Aztec civilization held fire opal sacred — the 'stone of the fire bird' associated with the hummingbird avatar of Huitzilopochtli. The Spanish conquest brought fire opal to Europe, where it was considered an exotic curiosity but never achieved the commercial success of the later Australian market. M
